subtitle: The Death Fighter
summary: Koch, a small-town doctor with no formal research training, discovers rod-shaped bacilli in the blood of animals dead from anthrax. Working alone behind a partition in his office, he infects mice with the disease, invents the hanging-drop technique to grow the bacilli in pure culture outside the body, and proves through eight generations of cultivation that one specific microbe causes one specific disease -- a foundational achievement in the history of medicine.

segments.anchor_sentence: Koch was spending his evenings fussing with his new microscope, he was beginning to find out just the right amount of light to shoot up into its lens with the reflecting mirror, he was learning just how needful it was to have his thin glass slides shining clean—those bits of glass on which he liked to put drops of blood from the carcasses of sheep and cows, that had died of anthrax....

visual.scene_description: Koch peers through his compound brass microscope at blood from a dead sheep, discovering strange rod-shaped and thread-like forms drifting among the blood cells.
visual.animation_prompt: Simple linocut print style illustration, bold black outlines, flat muted colors in dusty rose and sage green and ochre, rough textured edges, hand-printed look on natural paper, children's book illustration, stocky man with thick dark mustache and round wire spectacles in wool waistcoat and rolled shirtsleeves hunched over a compound brass microscope on a small wooden desk, thin glass slides with dark blood drops beside him, oil lamp casting shadows, circular magnified inset showing tiny rod-shaped bacilli and tangled thread-like filaments drifting among round blood cells

segments.anchor_sentence: "I do not see a way yet of finding out whether these little sticks and threads are alive," he meditated, "but there are other things to learn about them...."

visual.scene_description: Koch works secretly behind a partition in his doctor's office, surrounded by cages of white mice, while patients wait on the other side of the wall.
visual.animation_prompt: Simple linocut print style illustration, bold black outlines, flat muted colors in dusty rose and sage green and ochre, rough textured edges, hand-printed look on natural paper, children's book illustration, small partitioned room in a 19th-century doctor's office, stocky man with thick dark mustache and round wire spectacles in wool waistcoat crouching near stacked wire cages of white mice, brass microscope on cluttered wooden table, glass slides and wood slivers scattered about, thin wooden partition wall separating him from a visible waiting room beyond

segments.anchor_sentence: So this foiled globe-trotter started on his strange explorations.

visual.scene_description: Koch carefully dips a wooden sliver into a drop of dark anthrax blood, then inserts it into a cut at the base of a white mouse's tail in his makeshift laboratory.
visual.animation_prompt: Simple linocut print style illustration, bold black outlines, flat muted colors in dusty rose and sage green and ochre, rough textured edges, hand-printed look on natural paper, children's book illustration, stocky man with thick dark mustache and round wire spectacles in rolled shirtsleeves at a rough wooden table, carefully holding a thin wood sliver dipped in dark blood near a small white mouse, glass slides and a brass microscope nearby, homemade equipment of wood and sealing wax visible, dim oil lamp light in a cramped room

segments.anchor_sentence: "These threads must be alive," Koch pondered, "the sliver that I put into the mouse has a drop of blood on it and that drop holds only a few hundreds of those sticks—and these have grown into billions in the short twenty-four hours in which the beast became sick and died...."

visual.scene_description: Koch struggles late at night with his homemade oil-lamp incubator, trying to grow anthrax rods in ox-eye fluid between glass slides, frustrated by contaminating microbes.
visual.animation_prompt: Simple linocut print style illustration, bold black outlines, flat muted colors in dusty rose and sage green and ochre, rough textured edges, hand-printed look on natural paper, children's book illustration, stocky man with thick dark mustache and round wire spectacles in shirtsleeves hunched over a crude wooden incubator heated by a smoky oil lamp, two flat glass slides held between his fingers, deep wrinkles on his furrowed brow, nighttime scene with single lamp casting long shadows across a cluttered desk with brass microscope

segments.anchor_sentence: Then one day a perfectly easy, a foolishly simple way to watch his rods grow flashed into Koch's head.

visual.scene_description: Koch watches in awe through his microscope as anthrax rods multiply and grow into tangled threads in his hanging-drop preparation -- a breakthrough moment in microbiology.
visual.animation_prompt: Simple linocut print style illustration, bold black outlines, flat muted colors in dusty rose and sage green and ochre, rough textured edges, hand-printed look on natural paper, children's book illustration, stocky man with thick dark mustache and round wire spectacles seated at brass microscope eyes wide with excitement, an inverted glass slide with a concave well visible on the stage, circular magnified inset showing rod-shaped bacilli elongating and multiplying into dense tangled thread-like filaments against a pale background

segments.anchor_sentence: Time, his office and its dull duties, his waiting and complaining patients—all of these things became nonsense, seemed of no account, were unreal to Koch whose head was now full of nothing but dreadful pictures of the tangled skeins of the anthrax threads.

visual.scene_description: Koch triumphantly examines the dissected mouse that proves his eighth-generation pure-cultured bacilli still cause anthrax, confirming that one microbe causes one disease.
visual.animation_prompt: Simple linocut print style illustration, bold black outlines, flat muted colors in dusty rose and sage green and ochre, rough textured edges, hand-printed look on natural paper, children's book illustration, stocky man with thick dark mustache and round wire spectacles leaning forward at his brass microscope with an expression of intense concentration, a row of eight small glass hanging-drop slides arranged on the desk beside him, a small dissected white mouse pinned on a wooden board, circular magnified inset showing dense rod-shaped bacilli swarming among tissue
